items.foco: Classification by BMI and abdominal obesity
items.title: Classification by BMI and abdominal obesity
items.contentHtml:
Classification by BMI and abdominal obesity

  • The body mass index is calculated as weight (kg) / height (m²). Classification: normal weight 18.5-24.9 kg/m², overweight 25-29.9 kg/m², grade I obesity 30-34.9 kg/m², grade II 35-39.9 kg/m², grade III ≥40 kg/m².
  • Abdominal obesity is defined by waist circumference ≥102 cm in men and ≥88 cm in women (criteria of the National Cholesterol Education Program Adult Treatment Panel III).
  • In Asians, the body mass index thresholds for overweight are ≥23 kg/m² and obesity ≥25 kg/m²; The risk waist circumference is ≥90 cm in men and ≥80 cm in women.

items.foco: Metabolic syndrome and its diagnostic criteria
items.title: Metabolic syndrome: diagnostic criteria
items.contentHtml:
Metabolic syndrome: diagnostic criteria

  • Metabolic syndrome is diagnosed with ≥3 of 5 criteria according to the National Cholesterol Education Program Adult Treatment Panel III.
  • Criteria: waist circumference ≥102 cm (men) or ≥88 cm (women); triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL or treatment; high-density lipoprotein cholesterol <40 mg/dL (men) or <50 mg/dL (women); blood pressure ≥130/85 mmHg or treatment; fasting glucose ≥100 mg/dL or treatment.
  • Insulin resistance is the central pathophysiological axis; Metabolic syndrome doubles the risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus and quintuples that of cardiovascular disease.

items.title: Comorbidities: diabetes, high blood pressure, dyslipidemia
items.contentHtml:
Comorbidities: diabetes, high blood pressure, dyslipidemia

  • Obesity is the main risk factor for type 2 diabetes mellitus due to insulin resistance and beta cell dysfunction; Weight loss ≥5% improves glycemic control.
  • Hypertension in obesity is related to hyperactivation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system and increased plasma volume; The first line of therapy is usually an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor or an angiotensin II receptor antagonist.
  • Typical dyslipidemia is elevated triglycerides, low high-density lipoprotein cholesterol and small, dense low-density lipoprotein particles (atherogenic pattern).

items.title: Indications for bariatric surgery
items.contentHtml:
Indications for bariatric surgery

  • Main indication: body mass index ≥40 kg/m² or ≥35 kg/m² with major comorbidity (type 2 diabetes mellitus, arterial hypertension, obstructive sleep apnea, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis).
  • Optimal age 18-65 years; Failure of supervised medical treatment ≥6 months and absence of psychiatric or medical contraindications is required.
  • Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy are the most common procedures; Surgery achieves remission of type 2 diabetes mellitus in 60-80% of cases.

items.title: Anti-obesity drugs: liraglutide and orlistat
items.contentHtml:
Anti-obesity drugs: liraglutide and orlistat

  • Liraglutide is an analogue of glucagon-like peptide 1, dose for obesity: 3 mg/day subcutaneous (different from the dose for diabetes, which is 1.8 mg/day).
  • Orlistat is a lipase inhibitor. pancreatic; dose: 120 mg three times a day with meals containing fat; reduces fat absorption by ~30%, causing steatorrhea if fat intake is high.
  • Both require caloric deficit and exercise; liraglutide is more effective in weight loss (~5-10% of body weight).

items.title: Evaluation of obstructive sleep apnea in obesity
items.contentHtml:
Evaluation of obstructive sleep apnea in obesity

  • All patients with obesity and symptoms (snoring, breathing pauses, daytime sleepiness) should be screened with STOP-Bang questionnaire (≥3 points indicates high risk).
  • The definitive diagnosis is made with nocturnal polysomnography; the apnea-hypopnea index ≥5 events/hour confirms obstructive sleep apnea.
  • Obesity is the main risk factor for fatty deposits in the upper airway; Weight loss ≥10% reduces the apnea-hypopnea index significantly.

items.foco: Initial management with diet and exercise
items.title: Initial management: diet and exercise
items.contentHtml:
Initial management: diet and exercise

  • Initial goal: weight loss 5-10% of body weight in 6 months, with a caloric deficit of 500-750 kcal/day.
  • Recommended diet: balanced hypocaloric (proteins 15-20%, fats <30%, carbohydrates 45-55%); The Mediterranean diet has additional cardiovascular benefit.
  • Exercise: ≥150 minutes/week of moderate aerobic activity (e.g. brisk walking) plus resistance training 2-3 days/week to preserve muscle mass.

items.foco: Screening for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease
items.title: Screening for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease
items.contentHtml:
Screening for non-alcoholic hepatic steatosis

  • Screening with abdominal ultrasound or fatty liver index (Fatty Liver Index) is recommended in patients with obesity or metabolic syndrome.
  • non-alcoholic hepatic steatosis is defined by fatty infiltration of ≥5% of hepatocytes without significant alcohol consumption (<20 g/day in women, <30 g/day in men).
  • To differentiate non-alcoholic steatohepatitis from simple steatosis, the FIB-4 index (age, aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, platelets) or liver biopsy is used in selected cases.

items.foco: Hyperphagia and mutations in the MC4R gene
items.title: Hyperphagia and mutations in the MC4R gene
items.contentHtml:
Hyperphagia and mutations in the MC4R gene

  • The mutation in the melanocortin 4 receptor gene is the most common monogenic cause of early-onset severe obesity (prevalence 2-5% in extreme obesity).
  • Clinically: hyperphagia (insatiable appetite), hyperinsulinemia, increase in lean mass and normal or high height; autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance.
  • The diagnosis is clinical and is confirmed with gene sequencing; Patients partially respond to liraglutide, but not to unapproved melanocortin 4 receptor agonists.

items.foco: Screening for hypothyroidism and Cushing's syndrome
items.title: Screening for hypothyroidism and Cushing's syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Screening for hypothyroidism and Cushing's syndrome

  • screening for hypothyroidism with thyroid-stimulating hormone is recommended in all patients with obesity, especially if there is fatigue, cold intolerance or goiter; Untreated hypothyroidism can make weight loss difficult.
  • Cushing's syndrome is suspected in central obesity, violaceous stretch marks, high blood pressure and diabetes mellitus; initial screening with free cortisol in 24-hour urine or suppression test with 1 mg of dexamethasone.
  • Exogenous (simple) obesity generally does not present with elevated cortisol; Confirmation of Cushing's syndrome requires second-line testing (circadian rhythm, high-dose dexamethasone).

items.title: Increased cardiovascular risk in obesity
items.contentHtml:
Increased cardiovascular risk in obesity

  • Obesity increases the risk of coronary heart disease, stroke and heart failure; The risk is proportional to body mass index and waist circumference.
  • The obesity paradox describes that in heart failure or established chronic kidney disease, a high body mass index is associated with better survival, but does not imply the benefit of gaining weight.
  • Weight loss reduces blood pressure, improves the lipid profile and insulin sensitivity, reducing overall cardiovascular risk.

items.title: Sarcopenic obesity in older adults
items.contentHtml:
Sarcopenic obesity in older adults

  • Sarcopenic obesity combines body mass index ≥30 kg/m² with low muscle mass (sarcopenia) and low muscle strength (e.g. grip strength <27 kg in men, <16 kg in women).
  • Diagnosis requires dual X-ray absorptiometry to measure body composition; Appendicular muscle mass index <7 kg/m² (men) or <5.5 kg/m² (women) suggests sarcopenia.
  • Management includes hypocaloric diet with high protein content (≥1.2 g/kg/day) and progressive resistance exercise to preserve or increase muscle mass while losing fat.
